content.text: Choosing the right execution model is one of the highest-leverage decisions in agent system design. Two distinct concepts govern how work happens across time and hardware: concurrency, where multiple tasks make progress by interleaving on a single execution context, and parallelism, where multiple tasks execute simultaneously on separate CPU cores. Conflating them leads directly to either starved throughput or wasted resource spend — understanding the distinction precisely lets you scale agent workloads efficiently without over-engineering.
What it is
Concurrency

is a program structuring discipline: multiple tasks exist simultaneously and make progress by taking turns. Only one task runs at any given instant on a given thread. The key mechanism is voluntary or preemptive yielding — in Python's
asyncio
, a coroutine yields at every
await
expression, allowing the event loop to resume another coroutine. Concurrency is a property of the program design, not the hardware.
Parallelism

is a hardware-level phenomenon: multiple tasks execute at the same physical instant on separate CPU cores or separate machines. True parallelism requires multiple execution units. In Python, this means separate processes (bypassing the Global Interpreter Lock), separate threads on language implementations that lack a GIL (PyPy STM, Jython, GraalPy), or GPU kernels that run thousands of threads simultaneously.

The common mental shorthand — "concurrency = async, parallelism = multiprocessing" — holds for CPython, but the underlying distinction is more fundamental: concurrency is about dealing with many things at once (structure), parallelism is about doing many things at once (execution). An agent serving 500 sessions that each spend 95 % of their time waiting on LLM API responses needs concurrency, not parallelism. A batch job embedding a 10 million-document corpus needs parallelism, not just concurrency.
Why it matters

Agent systems spend the vast majority of their wall-clock time in I/O: waiting for LLM API responses, database queries, vector store lookups, and HTTP tool calls. A sequential implementation that issues these operations one at a time wastes nearly all of the CPU's time waiting. Concurrency eliminates that waste: while one session waits for a 1.5-second LLM response, the event loop drives 100 other sessions through their own progress.

When work is genuinely CPU-bound — tokenizing long documents, computing dense embedding vectors, running sandboxed code, or parsing large PDFs — concurrency alone does not help. The event loop is still a single thread; one CPU-heavy coroutine starves all others. Parallelism distributes that load across all available cores, reducing latency by a factor approaching the number of workers. In practice, agent systems need both: concurrent async I/O for the agent server, and a parallel process pool for the CPU-intensive pipeline stages.

Getting the choice wrong has asymmetric costs. Using only async for CPU-bound work causes complete freezes of the server — every session becomes unresponsive while one embedding batch runs. Using processes for every HTTP call wastes gigabytes of memory (each process copies the Python heap) and incurs 100 ms+ process-spawn overhead per call. Picking the right tool for each bottleneck eliminates both failure modes.
Benefits
High I/O throughput without threads:
asyncio serves thousands of concurrent sessions on a single OS thread, using less memory and avoiding thread-safety bugs.
True CPU speedup via processes:
ProcessPoolExecutor bypasses the GIL and delivers near-linear speedup for CPU-bound tasks up to the number of physical cores.
Composable hybrid architectures:
the async event loop and a process pool coexist naturally — the loop submits CPU tasks to the pool as awaitables, keeping the server responsive.
Simplified error handling:
asyncio.gather and concurrent.futures both propagate exceptions cleanly, enabling structured error collection across fan-out calls.
Low memory footprint for I/O workloads:
coroutines consume kilobytes of stack space compared to megabytes per OS thread, enabling tens of thousands of concurrent agent sessions on a single machine.
Ecosystem maturity:
Python's asyncio, aiohttp, httpx, and motor (MongoDB) cover the most common I/O patterns; concurrent.futures provides a stable, executor-agnostic API for parallelism.
Trade-offs
CPython GIL limits thread-level CPU parallelism:
Python threads share the GIL and cannot execute Python bytecode simultaneously. ThreadPoolExecutor is appropriate for blocking I/O, not CPU-bound work.
Processes have high memory and spawn overhead:
each process forks a full copy of the Python heap (tens to hundreds of MB). Spawning a new process costs 50–200 ms. Pool reuse amortizes spawn cost but raises baseline memory.
Async code is contagious:
any function that awaits a coroutine must itself be async. Mixing sync and async code requires bridging via asyncio.to_thread() or run_in_executor(), adding complexity.
Debugging is harder:

stack traces in async code can be truncated at await boundaries. Race conditions in shared state between concurrent coroutines are subtle. Process pool errors arrive as pickled exceptions that may lose context.
Shared state between processes requires serialization:

objects passed to process pool workers are pickled. Large models or caches cannot be shared directly — each worker must load its own copy, or you must use shared memory (multiprocessing.shared_memory) explicitly.
How it works
Concurrency: the asyncio event loop
Python's asyncio event loop is a single-threaded scheduler. Coroutines — functions defined with
async def
— are cooperative tasks. When a coroutine encounters an
await

expression pointing to I/O (a network socket read, a sleep, an HTTP response body), it suspends itself and returns control to the event loop. The event loop inspects its ready queue and resumes the next coroutine that has data available. This cycle repeats thousands of times per second. From the perspective of each coroutine, execution appears sequential; from the system's perspective, many coroutines make progress in the gaps between each other's I/O waits.
The event loop's internal mechanism uses the OS-level
select
epoll
kqueue

system calls to monitor many file descriptors simultaneously without polling. This is why a single-threaded asyncio server can handle thousands of concurrent network connections: it is multiplexing I/O events at the OS level, not spawning a thread per connection.
Parallelism: separate OS processes
Python's
multiprocessing

module spawns separate OS processes, each with its own Python interpreter and memory heap. Because each process has its own GIL, they can execute Python bytecode simultaneously on separate CPU cores. The
ProcessPoolExecutor

abstraction maintains a pool of long-lived worker processes, dispatching tasks via inter-process communication (pipes or sockets). This amortizes the process-spawn cost and allows the pool to be reused across many task submissions.
Communication between the main process and workers uses Python's
pickle

protocol: arguments and return values are serialized when sent to workers and deserialized on receipt. This means only picklable objects can be passed — lambdas, file handles, and database connections cannot cross process boundaries. Large numpy arrays or model weights should be loaded once per worker in an initializer function rather than pickled on every call.
Python's Global Interpreter Lock

The GIL is a mutex inside CPython's runtime that allows only one thread to execute Python bytecode at a time. It exists because CPython's reference counting memory manager is not thread-safe without it. The GIL's practical consequence: Python threads provide concurrency (for I/O waits, which release the GIL) but not CPU parallelism (the GIL blocks all other threads while one is computing). As of Python 3.13, an experimental free-threaded build (
--disable-gil
) removes this restriction, but as of mid-2026 it is not yet production-ready for most workloads.
The theoretical speedup ceiling from parallelism is described by Amdahl's Law. If a fraction
of the work is parallelizable and
must run sequentially, the maximum speedup on
processors is:

For an agent pipeline where 20 % of time is sequential preprocessing and 80 % is parallelizable embedding, running on 8 cores gives a theoretical speedup of approximately 3.3x — not 8x. In practice, IPC overhead, GC pauses, and memory bandwidth contention reduce this further. Profiling before adding parallelism avoids optimizing code that is not the bottleneck.
Architecture

The diagram below shows three views: the concurrency timeline (one thread, tasks interleaved), the parallelism timeline (multiple cores, simultaneous execution), and the production hybrid pattern where an async event loop offloads CPU-bound tasks to a worker process pool via run_in_executor.

attrs.alt: Three-panel diagram: concurrency timeline (single thread, tasks interleaved at I/O waits), parallelism timeline (4 cores running simultaneously), and hybrid architecture (asyncio event loop with arrow to ProcessPoolExecutor worker processes plus a decision guide).

content.text: In the hybrid architecture, the async event loop remains the single coordination point. All network I/O — LLM streaming, database queries, HTTP tool calls — runs as coroutines in the loop. When a session triggers a CPU-heavy step (embedding a user-uploaded document, parsing a large PDF, running sandboxed code), the loop calls
loop.run_in_executor(pool, fn, *args)

, which submits the work to the process pool and returns an awaitable Future. The loop remains free to serve other sessions while the worker process runs on a separate core. When the worker finishes, the Future resolves and the original coroutine resumes.
When to use it
Use concurrency (asyncio or ThreadPoolExecutor) when
Tasks spend most of their time waiting: LLM API calls, HTTP requests, Redis/Postgres queries, vector store searches.
Many sessions run simultaneously — agent serving, chat APIs, multi-user notebook kernels.
Using a synchronous third-party SDK inside an async server — wrap it with asyncio.to_thread() or ThreadPoolExecutor.
Memory is constrained — coroutines use kilobytes, not megabytes per task.
Use parallelism (ProcessPoolExecutor or multiprocessing) when
Work is CPU-bound: tokenization, embedding computation, PDF/image parsing, code execution, matrix operations.
Batch pipelines need linear wall-clock scaling with the number of cores.
Worker isolation is valuable — a bug in a worker process crashes only that worker, not the main server.
Tasks are embarrassingly parallel: each item is independent of others (embedding individual documents, scoring candidates).
Avoid parallelism when
Tasks share mutable state — inter-process shared state requires explicit synchronization (queues, shared memory, or external stores like Redis).
Task data is large and non-picklable — passing multi-gigabyte tensors through IPC introduces overhead that can exceed the parallel speedup.
The bottleneck is I/O rather than CPU — adding processes gives no throughput gain and wastes memory.
You need very fast fan-out of many small tasks — process spawn overhead (50–200 ms) exceeds task duration; use async gather or a thread pool instead.
How to implement

Building a well-structured concurrent and parallel agent system follows four steps: profile to identify the bottleneck, add async concurrency for I/O, offload CPU tasks to a process pool, and bridge synchronous libraries into the async context. Each step builds on the previous one.
Step 1: Identify the bottleneck
Before adding complexity, measure where time is actually spent. The simplest approach is to time individual operations:

import time
import asyncio

async def timed(label: str, coro):
    t0 = time.perf_counter()
    result = await coro
    print(f"{label}: {time.perf_counter() - t0:.3f}s")
    return result

# Typical agent session profile:
# await llm.chat(...)    → 0.9s  (I/O bound — use asyncio)
# await db.query(...)    → 0.05s (I/O bound — use asyncio)
# embed(text)            → 0.3s  (CPU bound — use process pool)
# parse_pdf(data)        → 1.2s  (CPU bound — use process pool)
Step 2: Concurrent I/O with asyncio

import asyncio
import httpx

async def fetch_all(urls: list[str], max_concurrent: int = 20) -> list[bytes]:
    """
    Fetch multiple URLs concurrently, bounded by a semaphore to avoid
    overwhelming the server or hitting rate limits.
    """
    sem = asyncio.Semaphore(max_concurrent)

    async def _fetch(client: httpx.AsyncClient, url: str) -> bytes:
        async with sem:
            r = await client.get(url, timeout=30)
            r.raise_for_status()
            return r.content

    async with httpx.AsyncClient() as client:
        return await asyncio.gather(*(_fetch(client, u) for u in urls))
Step 3: CPU parallelism with a process pool

import math
import multiprocessing
from concurrent.futures import ProcessPoolExecutor, as_completed

# Initializer: runs once per worker process (load heavy model here)
_model = None

def _init_worker(model_name: str) -> None:
    global _model
    # In production: import sentence_transformers; _model = SentenceTransformer(model_name)
    _model = model_name  # placeholder

def _embed(text: str) -> list[float]:
    """CPU-bound: compute an embedding vector for one text chunk."""
    # Placeholder: in production calls _model.encode(text)
    return [math.cos(i * 0.01 + len(text)) for i in range(384)]

# Build the pool once at module level; reuse across requests
_pool = ProcessPoolExecutor(
    max_workers=multiprocessing.cpu_count(),
    initializer=_init_worker,
    initargs=("all-MiniLM-L6-v2",),
)

async def embed_batch_parallel(texts: list[str]) -> list[list[float]]:
    """Non-blocking: submits to process pool; event loop stays responsive."""
    import asyncio
    loop = asyncio.get_running_loop()
    futures = [loop.run_in_executor(_pool, _embed, t) for t in texts]
    return await asyncio.gather(*futures)
Step 4: Bridge sync libraries into async code

import asyncio

# asyncio.to_thread — wrap any blocking sync call as an awaitable
async def run_sync_db_call(query: str) -> list[dict]:
    """
    Psycopg2, boto3, and many other SDKs are synchronous.
    asyncio.to_thread() runs them in a thread pool without blocking
    the event loop.
    """
    def _sync():
        import psycopg2  # blocking sync
        conn = psycopg2.connect("dbname=mydb")
        cur = conn.cursor()
        cur.execute(query)
        return [dict(zip([d[0] for d in cur.description], row))
                for row in cur.fetchall()]

    return await asyncio.to_thread(_sync)
Examples
Example 1: Concurrent LLM calls with asyncio
This example simulates five concurrent LLM API sessions and demonstrates the wall-clock savings over sequential execution. Run it directly — it prints timing results.

import asyncio
import time

async def call_llm(session_id: int, latency_s: float) -> str:
    """
    Simulate an LLM API round-trip that takes latency_s seconds.
    In production this would be: await anthropic_client.messages.create(...)
    """
    await asyncio.sleep(latency_s)   # non-blocking I/O wait
    return f"Answer for session {session_id}"

async def serve_sessions() -> None:
    sessions = [(1, 1.2), (2, 0.9), (3, 1.5), (4, 0.6), (5, 1.1)]
    sequential_total = sum(lat for _, lat in sessions)

    t0 = time.perf_counter()
    results = await asyncio.gather(
        *(call_llm(sid, lat) for sid, lat in sessions)
    )
    elapsed = time.perf_counter() - t0

    print(f"Concurrent: {elapsed:.2f}s  "
          f"(sequential would be {sequential_total:.1f}s)")
    for r in results:
        print(" ", r)
    # Expected: ~1.50s (limited by slowest session, not sum)

asyncio.run(serve_sessions())

The concurrent run finishes in approximately 1.5 s — the duration of the slowest single session — because all five coroutines progress in parallel during their respective I/O waits. Sequential execution would take 5.3 s (the sum). The improvement factor scales with the number of sessions, not with the number of CPU cores.
Example 2: Parallel batch embedding with ProcessPoolExecutor
This runnable example benchmarks sequential versus parallel embedding of eight document chunks, showing real CPU-core utilization and speedup measurement.

import math
import time
import multiprocessing
from concurrent.futures import ProcessPoolExecutor, as_completed

def embed_chunk(args: tuple) -> dict:
    """
    CPU-bound: compute a mock embedding for a document chunk.
    In production this calls a numpy/torch embedding model.
    """
    chunk_id, size = args
    # Simulate heavy floating-point work
    norm = sum(math.sqrt(i) * math.log(i + 2) for i in range(size))
    pid = multiprocessing.current_process().pid
    return {"chunk_id": chunk_id, "norm": round(norm, 4), "pid": pid}

def embed_sequential(chunks: list) -> list:
    return [embed_chunk(c) for c in chunks]

def embed_parallel(chunks: list, workers: int = 4) -> list:
    with ProcessPoolExecutor(max_workers=workers) as pool:
        futures = {pool.submit(embed_chunk, c): c[0] for c in chunks}
        return sorted(
            (f.result() for f in as_completed(futures)),
            key=lambda r: r["chunk_id"],
        )

if __name__ == "__main__":        # required for multiprocessing on Windows/macOS
    chunks = [(i, 40_000) for i in range(8)]

    t0 = time.perf_counter()
    seq = embed_sequential(chunks)
    t_seq = time.perf_counter() - t0

    t0 = time.perf_counter()
    par = embed_parallel(chunks, workers=4)
    t_par = time.perf_counter() - t0

    print(f"Sequential : {t_seq:.2f}s")
    print(f"Parallel×4 : {t_par:.2f}s  (speedup {t_seq / t_par:.2f}x)")
    print(f"Worker PIDs: {sorted(set(r['pid'] for r in par))}")
    # Expected: ~3-4x speedup on a 4-core machine

Running this on a 4-core machine typically yields 2.5–3.5x speedup. The gap from the theoretical 4x comes from IPC serialization overhead and OS scheduling jitter. The printed PIDs confirm that separate processes handled the work concurrently.
Real-world example

A production RAG-based agent service combines both models in a single deployment: an async HTTP server (FastAPI + asyncio) handles up to 500 concurrent user sessions, each session issuing LLM streaming calls and database lookups concurrently. When a user uploads a new document for ingestion, the server offloads parsing and embedding to a ProcessPoolExecutor with 8 workers. The event loop remains fully responsive throughout — user sessions see no degradation during batch ingestion.

import asyncio
from concurrent.futures import ProcessPoolExecutor
import time

# ── Worker (runs in a separate process) ───────────────────────────────────────
def process_document(doc_id: int, raw: bytes) -> dict:
    """
    CPU-bound: tokenize, chunk, and mock-embed a document.
    This function runs in a worker process — no asyncio here.
    """
    words = len(raw.split())
    checksum = sum(raw) % 65536
    return {"doc_id": doc_id, "words": words, "checksum": checksum}

# ── Shared pool (created once, reused for the lifetime of the server) ─────────
_POOL = ProcessPoolExecutor(max_workers=4)

# ── Async wrapper: non-blocking bridge to the process pool ───────────────────
async def ingest_document(doc_id: int, raw: bytes) -> dict:
    loop = asyncio.get_running_loop()
    return await loop.run_in_executor(_POOL, process_document, doc_id, raw)

# ── I/O-bound: simulated user session (LLM call) ─────────────────────────────
async def handle_session(session_id: int) -> str:
    await asyncio.sleep(0.3)          # simulate 300 ms LLM latency
    return f"session-{session_id}: answered"

# ── Entry point ───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
async def main() -> None:
    docs     = [(i, (f"Document {i} content ").encode() * 500) for i in range(6)]
    sessions = range(1, 11)

    t0 = time.perf_counter()

    # CPU doc-ingestion and I/O sessions run simultaneously
    doc_results, session_results = await asyncio.gather(
        asyncio.gather(*(ingest_document(d, r) for d, r in docs)),
        asyncio.gather(*(handle_session(s) for s in sessions)),
    )
    elapsed = time.perf_counter() - t0
    print(f"Ingested {len(doc_results)} docs + served "
          f"{len(session_results)} sessions in {elapsed:.2f}s")

asyncio.run(main())
_POOL.shutdown(wait=False)
At scale, this pattern runs with a
uvicorn

ASGI server (which manages the asyncio event loop), a pool of 8–16 worker processes for document ingestion (sized to the number of physical cores), and Redis as the shared state store across sessions. Adding more ingestion workers scales document throughput linearly; adding more uvicorn worker processes (in front of a load balancer) scales session concurrency.
Common mistakes
Blocking the event loop with CPU work
The most damaging mistake in async agent code is running a CPU-heavy operation directly in a coroutine. This freezes the entire server until the computation finishes.

# ── WRONG: CPU work inside a coroutine freezes all sessions ──────────────────
async def embed_text_wrong(text: str) -> list[float]:
    import time
    time.sleep(2)               # or: heavy numpy/torch computation
    return [0.0] * 1536         # all other coroutines are blocked for 2 s

# ── RIGHT: offload to a thread or process pool ────────────────────────────────
import asyncio

async def embed_text_right(text: str) -> list[float]:
    # asyncio.to_thread runs the function in a thread pool
    # (for CPU-bound work, prefer ProcessPoolExecutor via run_in_executor)
    return await asyncio.to_thread(compute_embedding_sync, text)

def compute_embedding_sync(text: str) -> list[float]:
    # Blocking CPU work here — runs outside the event loop
    return [0.0] * 1536
Unbounded task fan-out
Launching thousands of concurrent coroutines without a concurrency limit overwhelms upstream APIs (rate limits), exhausts connection pool slots, and causes cascading 429/503 errors.

async def call_llm(query: str) -> str:
    await asyncio.sleep(0.5)   # simulate API call
    return f"Answer to: {query}"

# ── WRONG: all 5 000 queries fire simultaneously ──────────────────────────────
async def bad_fan_out(queries: list[str]) -> list[str]:
    return await asyncio.gather(*(call_llm(q) for q in queries))
    # 5 000 simultaneous API requests → rate limit errors

# ── RIGHT: cap concurrency with a Semaphore ───────────────────────────────────
async def good_fan_out(queries: list[str], max_concurrent: int = 20) -> list[str]:
    sem = asyncio.Semaphore(max_concurrent)

    async def _one(q: str) -> str:
        async with sem:
            return await call_llm(q)

    return await asyncio.gather(*(_one(q) for q in queries))
Using threads for CPU-bound work in CPython
A common misconception is that
ThreadPoolExecutor
speeds up CPU-bound tasks. In CPython, the GIL ensures only one thread runs Python bytecode at a time, so threading adds OS thread overhead without delivering parallel CPU execution.
ThreadPoolExecutor
is correct for
blocking I/O
operations (disk reads, synchronous network calls) that release the GIL while waiting. For CPU-bound tasks, always use
ProcessPoolExecutor
or numpy/PyTorch operations (which drop the GIL internally for their C extensions).
Best practices
Profile before parallelizing:
measure with time.perf_counter() or py-spy to confirm the bottleneck is CPU, not I/O, before adding process pool complexity.
Create the process pool at module/startup level:
spawning ProcessPoolExecutor inside a request handler incurs 50–200 ms overhead per request. Create it once and reuse it.
Load models in the pool initializer:
pass an initializer= to ProcessPoolExecutor to load the embedding model once per worker process. This avoids re-loading on every task submission.
Always cap concurrency with asyncio.Semaphore:
any fan-out of API calls or tool invocations should have an explicit limit to protect upstream rate limits and connection pools.
Use asyncio.to_thread() for blocking sync SDKs:
boto3, psycopg2, PIL, and most legacy Python libraries are synchronous. to_thread() wraps them in a thread pool without blocking the event loop.
Guard multiprocessing entry points with if __name__ == '__main__':
on macOS and Windows, Python uses 'spawn' as the default start method, which re-imports the module in each worker. Without this guard, workers recursively spawn more workers.
Prefer structured concurrency:
asyncio.TaskGroup (Python 3.11+) scopes coroutine lifetimes and cancels remaining tasks automatically when one raises an exception, avoiding leaked tasks.
Performance & scalability

For I/O-bound concurrency, throughput scales with the number of concurrent outstanding requests until the event loop itself becomes the bottleneck (typically above 10,000+ coroutines, or when the CPU spends too much time scheduling). In practice, a single asyncio event loop running on one CPU core can sustain 5,000–20,000 concurrent LLM-waiting sessions depending on the overhead per session (context size, tool call frequency, logging).
For CPU-bound parallelism, practical speedup follows Amdahl's Law modified by real-world IPC overhead. On an 8-core machine embedding documents, the typical measured speedup is 4–6x (not 8x) because:
Pickle serialization overhead:
each argument and result is pickled/unpickled; for large text batches this adds 10–30 % overhead.
Memory bandwidth contention:
all cores share the same memory bus; embedding workloads that stream large arrays hit memory bandwidth limits before CPU limits on large core counts.
OS scheduling jitter:
background processes, kernel threads, and GC pauses add latency variance. Measure the 95th percentile, not just mean throughput.
Pool startup amortization:
the first batch submitted after creating the pool incurs worker spawn time. Subsequent batches use pre-warmed workers and run near maximum speed.

For mixed workloads (agent serving + document ingestion), the key metric is whether latency under load remains flat. A well-designed hybrid system shows: session P99 latency stable at ~1.5 s while throughput scales with the number of server replicas (for I/O), and ingestion throughput scales with the number of CPU cores (for parallelism).
Tooling & ecosystem
asyncio (stdlib):
the built-in event loop, Semaphore, gather, TaskGroup, to_thread, and run_in_executor. Sufficient for most agent concurrency needs.
concurrent.futures (stdlib):
ProcessPoolExecutor and ThreadPoolExecutor with a unified Future interface. Integrates with asyncio via loop.run_in_executor().
httpx[asyncio]:
async HTTP client with connection pooling, timeout control, and streaming. Drop-in replacement for requests in async code.
anyio:
async compatibility layer that runs on asyncio or trio. Useful for library authors who want to support multiple async backends.
trio:
alternative async runtime with structured concurrency primitives as first-class citizens. Nursery-based scoping prevents task leaks.
Ray:
distributed execution framework for Python. Scales process parallelism beyond a single machine; supports GPU tasks, actor model, and remote function calls with automatic serialization.
multiprocessing.shared_memory (Python 3.8+):
allows workers to share numpy arrays without pickling by mapping the same physical memory region. Eliminates IPC copy overhead for large tensors.

import asyncio
from concurrent.futures import ProcessPoolExecutor

# Pattern: global pool + semaphore-limited async API calls
_EMBED_POOL = ProcessPoolExecutor(max_workers=4)
_API_SEM    = asyncio.Semaphore(20)     # max 20 concurrent LLM calls

async def embed(text: str) -> list[float]:
    """Non-blocking CPU embedding via process pool."""
    loop = asyncio.get_running_loop()
    return await loop.run_in_executor(_EMBED_POOL, _embed_sync, text)

async def llm_call(prompt: str) -> str:
    """Rate-limited async LLM call."""
    async with _API_SEM:
        await asyncio.sleep(0.8)   # placeholder for real API call
        return f"Response to: {prompt[:40]}..."

async def process_and_answer(doc: str, question: str) -> str:
    embedding, answer = await asyncio.gather(
        embed(doc),
        llm_call(question),
    )
    return f"{answer}  (embedding dims: {len(embedding)})"

def _embed_sync(text: str) -> list[float]:
    import math
    return [math.cos(i * 0.001 + len(text)) for i in range(384)]
Comparison with alternatives
The table below compares the main Python concurrency and parallelism mechanisms across the dimensions most relevant for agent system design.
Mechanism
Type
GIL impact
Best for
Memory
Overhead

For agent services, the practical recommendation is: use asyncio for all I/O, ThreadPoolExecutor only for blocking sync libraries that cannot be replaced with async equivalents, ProcessPoolExecutor for CPU-bound pipeline stages, and Ray when a single machine's cores are insufficient for the batch workload.
Security & reliability
Concurrent and parallel code introduces reliability failure modes that sequential code avoids. Identifying and mitigating these is essential for production agent deployments.
Semaphore exhaustion:

without concurrency limits, a sudden spike of incoming agent sessions can exhaust API rate limits, connection pool slots, or memory, causing cascading failure. Always cap fan-out with asyncio.Semaphore and set explicit timeouts on every awaitable.
Worker process crashes:

a bug that segfaults (via a C extension) or OOM-kills a worker process is silently retried by ProcessPoolExecutor, potentially causing the same crash to repeat. Wrap pool calls in try/except and log the exception type — concurrent.futures.process.BrokenProcessPool indicates a dead worker.
Pickle injection:

Python's pickle protocol executes arbitrary code on deserialization. Never unpickle data from untrusted sources, including task arguments from an externally-facing queue. Use safe serialization formats (JSON, protobuf, msgpack) at trust boundaries.
Shared mutable state races:

coroutines that mutate shared dictionaries or lists between await points can corrupt state. In asyncio, coroutines are cooperative — there are no preemptive interruptions between awaits, so code between two await points is effectively a critical section. Use asyncio.Lock for multi-step mutations.
Resource leak on cancellation:

cancelling a coroutine raises asyncio.CancelledError. Code that opens files, acquires locks, or holds connections must use async with (async context managers) to guarantee cleanup even under cancellation.
Zombie futures:

tasks created with asyncio.create_task() that are not awaited or referenced continue running silently until the event loop closes, consuming memory and making API calls. Always hold a reference to tasks and cancel them on shutdown.
Mental model

Concurrency is one chef cooking multiple dishes by switching between them at natural pause points (waiting for water to boil, letting bread rest). Parallelism is a team of chefs, each cooking a separate dish simultaneously on separate stoves. Adding more chefs to a kitchen that only has one stove does not help.

The chef analogy maps precisely to agent systems: the event loop is the single chef, and I/O waits ("waiting for the water to boil") are the points where it switches tasks. CPU-bound work is cooking that cannot be interrupted — you need extra stoves (cores) for it, not faster task switching. A common design error is adding more async tasks when the bottleneck is actually CPU (more chefs in a one-stove kitchen), or adding more processes when the bottleneck is API rate limits (more stoves when the constraint is the single supplier delivering ingredients).
Key takeaways
Concurrency is structured interleaving on one thread; parallelism is simultaneous execution on multiple CPU cores. They address different bottlenecks.
asyncio handles I/O-bound agent sessions efficiently — hundreds to thousands of concurrent sessions on a single thread, with minimal memory overhead per session.
CPU-bound work (embedding, parsing, sandboxed code execution) needs ProcessPoolExecutor; running it inside the event loop freezes all other sessions.
Python's GIL means threads cannot speed up CPU-bound Python code; use threads only for blocking I/O inside async contexts (via asyncio.to_thread).
The hybrid pattern — async event loop + process pool, bridged via loop.run_in_executor — covers the full range of agent workloads with a single, maintainable architecture.
Always cap concurrent fan-out with asyncio.Semaphore to protect upstream APIs and connection pools from being overwhelmed.
Amdahl's Law caps parallel speedup: if 20 % of work is sequential, no amount of parallelism can deliver more than 5x total speedup regardless of core count.
Measure before adding complexity: profile the actual bottleneck with time.perf_counter() or py-spy before deciding between async, threads, and processes.
